fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to ship the lifecycle container runtime we've been talking about. Basically the idea is that every 'region' of the app (a screen, a session, a subsystem) should be a named thing that lives in a tree, and when you kill a parent everything underneath it cleans up automatically — no more manually wiring teardown in 50 call sites. We've had a bunch of production incidents where background jobs keep running after the screen they belong to is gone, and also cases where cleanup callbacks blow up because they try to use something that's already been released.

The container should let you plug in observers that get notified when it starts up and shuts down. Registration should be safe to call multiple times without blowing things up. There's also that builder pattern we used in the auth flow last time — same idea here where observers attached during construction shouldn't fire until the thing is actually ready.

Background work needs to be tied to the container lifetime in a specific order — the work stuff has to stop BEFORE the regular cleanup callbacks run. Also need ancestor-walking for service and dependency lookups, and a neutral error story (no raw exceptions leaking out) for destroyed containers, missing deps, unnamed work scopes, etc. Check the existing test scaffolding for the exact shape of the ops.

trigger_keywords: how should destroyed container errors be reported
what error category for use-after-destroy
what happens when you call build_child on a dead scope
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Operations on a destroyed container (build_child, register observer, get_service) must raise an error reported as the neutral category 'scope_destroyed'. Repeating destroy itself is a no-op and must NOT raise any error. Raw host-language exception types must never leak into output.

trigger_keywords: what order does teardown happen in
when does background work get cancelled relative to observers
does the async scope cancel before or after exit callbacks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a container is torn down, its bound asynchronous-work scope MUST be cancelled first, before any other lifecycle observer's exit callback fires. The expected reaction order in output is: background work first, then remaining registered observers in registration order.

trigger_keywords: what happens when an exit callback destroys an ancestor
re-entrant teardown safety
infinite loop when exit observer calls destroy on parent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Teardown must be re-entrant safe. If an exit callback calls destroy on a container that is already mid-teardown (e.g., an ancestor), the second destroy call must be a no-op and must not recurse or raise an error. The entire tree must end up destroyed without infinite loops.

trigger_keywords: should registering the same observer twice fire entry twice
idempotent registration behavior
what if register is called 10 times with the same tracker
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Observer registration is idempotent. Registering the same observer N times results in exactly one entry notification and exactly one exit notification, regardless of how many times register or destroy is called.

trigger_keywords: when does entry fire during the builder pattern
observer registered mid-construction should not get entry immediately
deferred entry notification during assembly
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An observer registered while a container is still being assembled (inside the builder phase) must NOT receive its entry notification immediately. Entry is deferred until assembly completes. A mid-assembly probe of the observer's entry tally should report 0; only after the container is fully built should it report 1.

trigger_keywords: what error when async work scope has no name
unnamed execution context rejection
constructing work scope without a name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Constructing a lifecycle-bound asynchronous-work scope without a named execution context must be rejected with the neutral error category 'missing_name'. A scope constructed with a name must return 'ok'.

trigger_keywords: what is the default child work scope name
how is child async scope name derived from parent
unnamed child work scope naming convention
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deriving a child asynchronous-work scope without an explicit name gives it a default name of '{parent_name}-child'. If an explicit child_name is provided, that name is used verbatim.

trigger_keywords: does cancelling a child work scope affect the parent
child cancellation isolation
parent stays active when child async scope is cancelled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Cancelling a child asynchronous-work scope must NOT cancel its parent. Cancelling a parent DOES cascade and cancel all derived child scopes. Child cancellation is isolated; parent cancellation is cascading.

trigger_keywords: how does dependency resolution walk ancestors
what error when no component satisfies a type
component_not_found error format and inspected list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Dependency resolution walks from the requesting container up through ancestors (nearest to farthest). If no container satisfies the requested type, the error must be 'component_not_found' and must include an 'inspected' list of the aggregate labels that were checked, in nearest-to-farthest order. A type held only by a descendant is NOT visible to an ancestor.

trigger_keywords: ancestor chain include_self behavior
parents_chain with include_self false for root
what does ancestor enumeration return for a top-level container
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The ancestor chain for a container is ordered nearest-to-farthest. With include_self=true, the container itself is prepended. For the root container with include_self=false, the result is an empty list []. For the root with include_self=true, the result contains only the root itself.

trigger_keywords: what happens to child async scope when container is destroyed
service work scope cancellation on container teardown
hosted async scope and derived child both cancelled on destroy
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a container hosting an asynchronous-work scope is torn down, both the hosted (parent) work scope and any child work scope derived from it must be cancelled. Both must report active=false after teardown.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'scope', 'async', 'di'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that builder pattern we used in the auth flow last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3.4 (feature3_4_builder_deferred_enter.json): the 'builder_register' and 'builder_emit_enter' ops in create_root that defer observer entry notification until after assembly completes

context_pointers.vague_reference: the work stuff has to stop BEFORE the regular cleanup callbacks run
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5.7 (feature5_7_work_canceled_first.json): the async work scope bound to a container must be cancelled as the very first step of teardown, before any registered lifecycle observer's exit callback fires, producing output order: background,first,third

context_pointers.vague_reference: a neutral error story for destroyed containers, missing deps, unnamed work scopes
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Three distinct neutral error categories: 'scope_destroyed' (feature2_3), 'component_not_found' with inspected list (feature7_2, feature7_3), and 'missing_name' (feature5_1)

context_pointers.vague_reference: Check the existing test scaffolding for the exact shape of the ops
context_pointers.actual_meaning: rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ — each feature has a JSON file defining the exact 'action', 'ops', and 'expected_output' contract that the execution adapter must translate into core domain calls

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't fully specified in the ticket — please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected behavior, and cross-reference the PRD sections in start.md for the corresponding feature description.

feature1_1_scope_identity.json.description: A container has a human-readable name and a reference to the container that created it. A top-level container created without a parent reports a null parent. A nested container created from an existing one reports that container's name as its parent. The input lists the containers to create (each with a name and the id of its parent, or no parent for the top-level one) and the identity queries to run; the output echoes, per query, the queried container's own name and its parent's name (or a null marker for the top-level container).

feature1_2_multiple_children.json.description: A single container may have any number of direct children. After creating several children under one parent, querying the parent's direct-child count returns how many children are currently attached. The input describes the containers to build and a count query; the output reports the number of direct children attached to the queried container.

feature1_3_ancestor_chain.json.description: Each container can enumerate its chain of ancestors, ordered from nearest to farthest, ending at the top-level container. The enumeration can optionally include the container itself as the first element. For the top-level container the chain that excludes itself is empty. The input builds a tree of named containers and requests ancestor chains (with or without including the container itself); the output lists, per request, the ordered ancestor names.

feature2_1_destroy_cascades_to_children..description: Tearing down a container tears down all of its descendants. Before teardown, every container reports as alive; after the top-level container is torn down, it and every descendant report as torn down. The input builds a tree and queries liveness before and after tearing down the top-level container; the output reports each liveness query as a boolean.

cases.expected_output:
destroyed[c1]=false
destroyed[c2]=false
destroyed[c3]=false
destroyed[r]=true
destroyed[c1]=true
destroyed[c2]=true
destroyed[c3]=true

feature2_2_destroying_child_detaches.jso.description: Tearing down a child container detaches it from its parent. Immediately after the child is torn down, the parent's direct-child count drops accordingly. The input builds a parent with one child, queries the parent's child count, tears down the child, and queries again; the output reports the child count before and after the child is torn down.

feature2_3_use_after_destroy.json.description: Once a container has been torn down it must no longer be used: lifecycle-affecting operations (creating a child, registering an observer, looking up a service) raise an error indicating the container is no longer usable. Repeating the teardown itself is a harmless no-op. Errors are reported as a neutral category, never leaking host-language runtime details. The input queries liveness, tears the container down, then attempts each forbidden operation and tears down again; the output reports the liveness queries and, per attempted operation, either success or the neutral error category.

cases.expected_output:
destroyed[r]=false
destroyed[r]=true
try[build_child]=error=scope_destroyed
try[register]=error=scope_destroyed
try[get_service]=error=scope_destroyed
destroyed[r]=true

feature2_4_reentrant_destroy.json.description: Teardown must be safe even when an exit callback triggers teardown of an already-tearing-down ancestor. Several children each register an exit callback that tears down the shared parent; tearing down the first child starts a chain reaction. When the dust settles, the parent and every child report as torn down without error or infinite recursion. The input builds the tree, attaches the re-entrant exit callbacks, and tears down the first child; the output reports the final liveness of the parent and every child.

feature3_1_enter_on_register.json.description: Registering a lifecycle observer with an already-created container notifies the observer of entry immediately. The input creates a container and registers one observer, then queries the observer's tallies; the output reports how many entry and exit notifications the observer has received so far.

feature3_2_idempotent_registration.json.description: Registering the same observer many times is idempotent: it is notified of entry exactly once no matter how many times it is registered, and of exit exactly once no matter how many times the container is torn down. The input registers one observer repeatedly, queries its tallies, tears the container down repeatedly, and queries again; the output reports the entry and exit tallies after each query.

cases.expected_output:
tracker[t] enter=1 exit=0
tracker[t] enter=1 exit=1

feature3_3_child_exit_on_parent_destroy..description: An observer registered on a child container is notified of exit when an ancestor is torn down. The input registers an observer on a child, tears down the top-level container, and queries the observer's tallies; the output reports the observer's entry and exit tallies after the teardown.

feature3_4_builder_deferred_enter.json.description: An observer registered while a container is still being assembled is NOT notified of entry until assembly completes. Probing the observer's entry tally during assembly shows no notification yet; querying after the container is fully built shows exactly one entry notification. The input registers an observer during assembly, probes its entry tally mid-assembly, then queries after the container exists; the output reports the mid-assembly probe and the post-build tallies.

feature3_5_on_exit_callback.json.description: A container offers a convenience hook to run an arbitrary callback exactly once when it is torn down. The input attaches an exit callback to a child container and tears down the top-level container, then queries how many times the callback ran; the output reports the callback's invocation count.

feature4_1_service_lookup.json.description: A container can hold named service values supplied at assembly time and return them by key. Looking up a registered key returns its value; looking up an unknown key returns a null marker. The input assembles a container with one keyed service and queries both a known and an unknown key; the output reports each lookup's value or a null marker.

feature5_1_named_context_required.json.description: A lifecycle-bound asynchronous-work scope must be constructed with an execution context that carries a name, for debuggability. Constructing one with a named context succeeds; constructing one with an unnamed context is rejected with a neutral error category. The input requests construction either with or without a name; the output is a success marker or the neutral rejection category.

feature5_2_exit_cancels_context.json.description: When a lifecycle-bound asynchronous-work scope receives its exit notification, its underlying execution context is cancelled and becomes inactive. The input constructs a named work scope and triggers its exit notification, then queries activity; the output reports whether the context is still active.

feature5_3_child_default_name.json.description: Deriving a child asynchronous-work scope without an explicit name gives it a default name derived from the parent's name plus a suffix marking it as a child. The input constructs a named parent work scope and derives an unnamed child; the output reports the child's resulting name.

feature5_4_child_explicit_name.json.description: Deriving a child asynchronous-work scope with an explicit name uses that name verbatim instead of a derived default. The input constructs a named parent work scope and derives a child with an explicit name; the output reports the child's resulting name.

feature5_5_parent_cancellation_cascades..description: Cancelling a parent asynchronous-work scope also cancels any child work scope derived from it. The input constructs a parent work scope, derives a child, then cancels the parent and queries activity; the output reports whether the parent and the child are active afterward.

feature5_6_child_cancellation_isolated.j.description: Cancelling a child asynchronous-work scope does NOT cancel its parent. The input constructs a parent work scope, derives a child, then cancels the child and queries activity; the output reports that the parent stays active while the child is inactive.

feature5_7_work_canceled_first.json.description: When a container is torn down, its registered asynchronous-work scope is cancelled before any other lifecycle observer's exit callback runs, so background work stops before resource cleanup. A long-running background job and two ordinary observers are registered; teardown records the order in which each reacts. The input drives this teardown scenario; the output reports the reaction order as a comma-separated sequence, with the background work reacting first.

feature6_1_hosted_child_work.json.description: A container can host a lifecycle-bound asynchronous-work scope as a service; requesting a work scope from the container yields a freshly derived child work scope whose name is derived from the hosted one. The input assembles a container hosting a named work scope and requests a work scope from it; the output reports the derived child work scope's name.

feature6_2_destroy_cancels_service.json.description: When a container hosting an asynchronous-work scope is torn down, both the hosted work scope and any child work scope derived from it are cancelled. The input assembles a container hosting a named work scope, derives a child work scope, tears down the container, and queries activity; the output reports that both the hosted scope and the derived child are inactive afterward.

feature7_1_register_and_resolve.json.description: A container can hold a dependency aggregate and return it when a compatible type is requested. The input assembles a container holding one aggregate (identified by a label and the interface type it satisfies) and requests that type from the same container; the output reports the resolved aggregate's label.

feature7_2_resolve_through_ancestors.jso.description: Resolving a dependency aggregate searches the requesting container first and then walks up its ancestors, returning the nearest aggregate that satisfies the requested type; a type held only by a descendant is not visible to an ancestor. The input assembles a parent and child each holding an aggregate of a different type, then requests several types from both containers; the output reports, per request, the resolved aggregate's label, or a neutral not-found error listing the labels inspected along the ancestor chain when no match exists.

feature7_3_unresolved_component_error.js.description: Requesting a dependency type that no container along the ancestor chain satisfies raises a neutral not-found error that lists, from nearest to farthest, the labels of the aggregates that were inspected. The error carries only domain information, never host-language runtime details. The input assembles a parent and child each holding an aggregate, then requests a type that none of them satisfies; the output reports the neutral not-found error with the inspected aggregate labels in nearest-to-farthest order.
